Module 1
Fundamentals of Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence

A

AI → ML → DL → Generative AI hierarchy and distinctions

SageMaker, managed AI services, generative AI layer

Speed, scalability, integration, security

Cost Considerations
Latency, throughput, token pricing, custom model costs
Prerequisites
None — entry-level module
Leads To
AWS-specific AI services, model deployment, cost optimization
Module Introduction

Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence are no longer niche research topics — they are the foundational infrastructure of modern software systems. Before you can work with AWS AI services, deploy models, or optimize costs, you need a clear mental model of what these technologies actually are, how they relate to each other, and why they are structured the way they are.

This module builds that mental model from the ground up. You will trace the hierarchy from AI as a broad field all the way down to Generative AI as a specific capability, understand how machines learn from data, and see how AWS has organized its services to match that hierarchy. Every concept introduced here becomes a vocabulary anchor for the rest of the MasterClass.

A
The AI Ecosystem
A
A1 Defining the Field: AI, ML, DL, and Generative AI

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the broadest of the four terms. It refers to the general goal of enabling machines to simulate human-like intelligence — tasks like recognizing objects, understanding speech, making decisions, or solving problems. AI is the umbrella field; everything else lives inside it.

Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of AI. Rather than programming machines with explicit rules, ML enables systems to learn patterns directly from data. The machine improves its performance through exposure to examples, not through hand-crafted logic.

Deep Learning (DL) is a subset of ML. It uses artificial neural networks — computational structures loosely inspired by the layered structure of the human brain — to learn representations of data. Deep Learning excels at complex, high-dimensional tasks like image recognition and language understanding.

Generative AI is a subset of Deep Learning. Instead of classifying or predicting existing categories, generative models create entirely new content — text, images, audio, code — by learning the underlying patterns of their training data and generating novel samples from those patterns.

Critical Distinction

Generative AI creates. Traditional ML analyzes, classifies, or predicts. A model that tells you whether an email is spam is traditional ML. A model that writes the email is Generative AI. This distinction matters significantly for AWS exam questions.

A2 The Hierarchy: AI ⟶ ML ⟶ DL ⟶ Generative AI

These four concepts are not alternatives or competing approaches — they are nested layers. Each inner layer is a more specific implementation of the one above it. Understanding this nesting prevents a common confusion: Generative AI is not a replacement for ML, it is ML at a more capable and specialized level.

AI
Umbrella field — any technique enabling machine intelligence
ML
Learns from data — no explicit rules required
Deep Learning
Neural network-based ML — excels at perception tasks
Generative AI
DL that creates new data — text, images, audio, code
Key Points
  • AI ⊃ ML ⊃ Deep Learning ⊃ Generative AI — each is a proper subset of the one above.
  • The critical exam distinction: Generative AI creates; traditional ML analyzes or predicts.
  • All Generative AI systems are DL systems, but not all DL systems are generative.
B
Machine Learning Fundamentals
B
B1 The ML Pipeline

Building an ML model is not a single step — it is an iterative pipeline where each stage feeds into the next. Understanding this pipeline helps you reason about where models succeed or fail, and where AWS services fit in.

01
Data Collection
Gather raw data from relevant sources — databases, APIs, sensors, documents, web scrapes.
02
Data Preparation
Clean and preprocess: handle missing values, remove duplicates, normalize formats, split into training/validation/test sets.
03
Training
Feed prepared data to a learning algorithm. The model adjusts its internal parameters to minimize prediction error.
04
Evaluation
Measure model performance on held-out data using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1, or RMSE.
05
Iteration
Return to earlier stages — collect more data, adjust the algorithm, or tune hyperparameters — until performance targets are met.
Garbage In, Garbage Out

An ML model is only as good as its training data. High-quality, representative data is the single most important factor in model quality. Sophisticated algorithms applied to poor data produce poor models — every time.

B2 Types of Data

ML algorithms work with different kinds of data, and understanding these categories helps you choose the right algorithm and preprocessing approach.

Labeled Data
  • Each data point has a correct answer attached
  • Example: image tagged "cat" or "dog"
  • Required for supervised learning
  • Expensive to produce at scale
Unlabeled Data
  • Raw data with no attached answers
  • Example: raw web pages, audio recordings
  • Used in unsupervised and self-supervised learning
  • Much more abundant and cheaper to collect
Structured Data
  • Organized in predefined schema (rows and columns)
  • Tabular: customer records, financial data
  • Time-series: stock prices, sensor readings over time
Unstructured Data
  • No predefined format or schema
  • Text: emails, articles, code, social posts
  • Images/Video: photos, surveillance feeds
  • The majority of real-world data is unstructured
Key Points
  • Labeled vs. Unlabeled determines which learning type you can apply.
  • Structured vs. Unstructured determines which algorithms and preprocessing steps are appropriate.
B3 Learning Types

The three core learning paradigms define how a model uses its training data. The choice of paradigm is determined primarily by whether labeled data is available and what kind of problem you are solving.

Supervised Learning

The model learns from labeled data — it sees many (input, correct output) pairs and adjusts its parameters to predict the correct output for new, unseen inputs. Used for classification (spam/not spam, disease/healthy) and regression (predicting a price or temperature). This is the most widely-used paradigm for production ML today.

Unsupervised Learning

The model learns from unlabeled data by finding hidden structure — clusters, patterns, or compressed representations. No correct answers are provided. Used for customer segmentation, anomaly detection, and dimensionality reduction. Particularly useful when labeling is expensive or impossible.

Reinforcement Learning

The model — called an agent — learns through interaction with an environment. It receives rewards for desirable actions and penalties for undesirable ones, and gradually learns a policy that maximizes cumulative reward. Used for game playing, robotics, and autonomous systems. AWS DeepRacer is a hands-on implementation of this paradigm.

Semi-Supervised Learning [+]

A practical hybrid: a small amount of labeled data is combined with a large amount of unlabeled data. The model uses the labeled examples to guide pattern discovery on the larger unlabeled set. This is valuable in real-world scenarios where labeling every data point is cost-prohibitive — for example, medical image datasets.

Key Points
  • Supervised = labeled data, known output targets (classification, regression).
  • Unsupervised = unlabeled data, discover hidden structure (clustering, anomaly detection).
  • Reinforcement = no labels — agent learns from reward signals through trial and error.
  • Semi-supervised is a practical middle ground — small labeled set + large unlabeled set.
B4 Inference

Inference is the deployment phase of a model's life — the step where a trained model is applied to new, real-world data to make predictions. Training is what happens in the lab; inference is what happens in production. Understanding inference modes is important for AWS architecture decisions.

Batch Inference
  • Processes a large dataset all at once
  • Not time-sensitive — results can take minutes or hours
  • More cost-efficient for high-volume workloads
  • Example: overnight fraud scoring on all transactions
Real-Time Inference
  • Processes one request at a time, instantly
  • Requires low latency — milliseconds matter
  • Higher cost per prediction
  • Example: product recommendations as user browses
Key Points
  • Training happens once (or periodically). Inference happens continuously at scale.
  • Batch inference = higher throughput, lower cost, higher latency. Real-time = lower latency, higher cost.
C
Deep Learning and Neural Networks
C
C1 Artificial Neural Networks

Deep Learning is built on Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) — computational systems loosely inspired by how the biological brain processes information. The key word is "loosely": ANNs share structural principles with neurons (interconnected units that pass signals) but are vastly simplified mathematical abstractions, not simulations of brain biology.

A neural network consists of nodes (neurons) organized into layers, connected by weighted links. Learning occurs by adjusting the weights on those connections so the network's outputs become increasingly accurate over time.

Input Layer
Receives raw data — pixel values, token IDs, sensor readings. One node per input feature.
Hidden Layers
One or more intermediate layers that learn internal representations. "Deep" networks have many hidden layers. Each layer detects progressively more abstract patterns.
Output Layer
Produces the final result — a class label, a probability, a generated token, etc.
How Learning Works

During training, the network makes a prediction, compares it to the correct answer, computes the error, and uses a process called backpropagation to adjust weights throughout the network — reducing the error over thousands or millions of iterations. [+]

Key Points
  • Neural networks = nodes organized in layers (input → hidden → output), connected by weights.
  • "Deep" in Deep Learning refers to the number of hidden layers — more layers = more abstract features learned.
  • Learning = adjusting weights to reduce prediction error across many training examples.
C2 Applications: Computer Vision and NLP

Neural networks are especially powerful for two categories of tasks that require perceiving and interpreting complex, unstructured data.

Computer Vision (CV)
  • Understanding images and video
  • Image Classification — "What is in this image?"
  • Object Detection — "Where are objects, and what are they?"
  • Segmentation — "Which pixels belong to which object?"
  • AWS service: Amazon Rekognition
Natural Language Processing (NLP)
  • Understanding and generating human language
  • Text Classification — categorize documents
  • Sentiment Analysis — detect positive/negative tone
  • Machine Translation — convert between languages
  • Text Generation — produce coherent language
  • AWS service: Amazon Comprehend, Amazon Translate, Amazon Lex
D
Generative AI and Foundation Models
D
D1 What Generative AI Can Create

Generative AI is capable of producing text, images, audio, video, and code. The key enablers behind this capability are two things working together: massive computational power (GPUs, TPUs) and access to very large, diverse datasets. Neither alone is sufficient — the combination is what makes modern Generative AI possible.

D2 Foundation Models (FMs)

A Foundation Model (FM) is a large model pretrained on internet-scale data that can serve as a general-purpose starting point for a wide variety of tasks. The name captures the idea: the model provides a foundation on which more specific applications are built.

Before Foundation Models, building an AI system for a specific task typically required collecting task-specific data and training a specialized model from scratch. Foundation Models change this: instead of starting from zero, developers adapt an existing pretrained model to their use case — saving enormous amounts of time and compute.

Key FM Properties

Multi-task capability — a single FM can write code, summarize documents, answer questions, and translate text. General-purpose adaptability — FMs are not locked to one domain; they are fine-tuned or prompted for specific use cases. Internet-scale pretraining — trained on billions of tokens from diverse sources.

Amazon Bedrock

Amazon Bedrock is AWS's managed service that provides access to Foundation Models from leading providers — Anthropic (Claude), Meta (Llama), Mistral AI, Amazon (Titan), and others — through a single API. Developers access state-of-the-art FMs without managing the underlying infrastructure.

D3 The FM Lifecycle

Foundation Models go through a structured lifecycle from raw data to production deployment. Understanding each stage helps you reason about where customization and improvement happen.

01
Data Selection
Collect large, diverse, unlabeled datasets — web text, books, code repositories, scientific papers.
02
Pre-training (Self-Supervised)
The model is trained to predict masked or next tokens using the data's own structure as the label source — no human annotation required. This is why FMs can be trained at internet scale. Continuous Pre-Training extends this process to expand knowledge across additional domains.
03
Optimization
Apply techniques like fine-tuning or RLHF (covered in Section F) to align the model's behavior with desired outputs.
04
Evaluation
Measure quality using standardized benchmarks and task-specific metrics — accuracy, BLEU score, human preference ratings.
05
Deployment
Integrate into production systems — APIs, applications, internal tools. AWS Bedrock handles this for managed FMs.
06
Feedback Loop
Collect usage signals and user feedback to continuously improve subsequent model versions.
Critical Exam Point

FMs use self-supervised learning — they do not require human-labeled data for pretraining. The data's own structure generates the training signal. This is what makes internet-scale training feasible.

Key Points
  • Foundation Models = pretrained on massive unlabeled data, general-purpose, multi-task capable.
  • Self-supervised learning eliminates the need for manual labeling during pretraining.
  • Continuous Pre-Training expands an existing FM's knowledge to new domains without full retraining.
  • Amazon Bedrock provides managed access to FMs from multiple providers through a single API.
E
Generative Model Architectures
E
E1 Large Language Models (LLMs)

Large Language Models (LLMs) are generative models trained on vast quantities of text. Their primary capability is generating human-like text — answering questions, writing code, summarizing documents, conducting conversations. Most state-of-the-art LLMs are built on the Transformer architecture, introduced in the landmark "Attention Is All You Need" paper. [~]

To understand how LLMs process language, you need to understand three interconnected concepts:

Tokens
The smallest unit of text the model processes. A token is not always a complete word — it could be a word, a part of a word, a punctuation mark, or a space. Tokenization standardizes input so the model has a consistent vocabulary to work with.
Embeddings
Each token is converted to a numerical vector — a list of numbers that represents the token's meaning. Semantically related words produce vectors that are close together in vector space. "Cat," "feline," and "kitten" will have embeddings near each other; "cat" and "database" will not.
Vectors
The mathematical objects (arrays of numbers) that embeddings produce. Vector arithmetic on word embeddings captures semantic relationships — this is how models "understand" that concepts are related.
How They Work Together

Text → Tokenize into tokens → Embed each token as a vector → Transformer layers process the vectors to understand context and relationships → Generate the next token → Repeat until the response is complete. [+]

Key Points
  • LLMs are Transformer-based — the Transformer is the dominant architecture for text generation.
  • Tokens = basic processing units; Embeddings = numerical representations; Vectors = capture semantic relationships.
  • Semantically similar words have embeddings that are mathematically close in vector space.
E2 Diffusion Models

Diffusion models are the architecture behind most state-of-the-art image generation systems (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Midjourney). Their core idea is elegant: learn to reverse a noise-adding process so the model can generate clean, structured data from pure randomness.

Forward Diffusion (Training)
  • Start with a clean, real data sample
  • Gradually add random noise over many steps
  • Until the sample becomes pure noise
  • The model learns what noise was added at each step
Reverse Diffusion (Inference)
  • Start with pure random noise
  • Iteratively remove noise step by step
  • Guided by a text prompt or condition
  • Produces a coherent, generated image or sample
Key Points
  • Forward diffusion = noise addition (training phase). Reverse diffusion = noise removal (inference/generation phase).
  • Diffusion models are the leading architecture for high-quality image and audio generation.
E3 Multimodal Models

Multimodal models can process and generate across multiple types of data simultaneously — for example, understanding both an image and a text prompt together to produce a response. They are not limited to one modality (text-only, image-only) and can combine information across types.

Examples of Multimodal Combinations

Text + Image (answering questions about a photo) · Image + Audio (generating captions for video) · Text → Image (generating graphics from written instructions) · Image + Text → Text (visual question answering, image captioning)

E4 Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

GANs consist of two neural networks in adversarial competition with each other, trained simultaneously:

Generator
  • Creates synthetic (fake) data from random noise
  • Goal: produce fakes indistinguishable from real data
  • Gets better as the Discriminator catches its fakes
Discriminator
  • Receives both real data and Generator's fakes
  • Goal: correctly identify which is real and which is fake
  • Gets better as the Generator improves its fakes

Training converges when the Generator produces samples so realistic that the Discriminator can no longer reliably distinguish them from real data. GANs were historically significant for image synthesis but have been largely superseded by Diffusion models for image generation tasks due to training stability issues. [+]

Key Points
  • GAN = two competing networks: Generator (creates fakes) vs. Discriminator (detects fakes).
  • Training success = Generator produces indistinguishable fakes. The competition drives both networks to improve.
E5 Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)

Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) combine an autoencoder structure (Encoder → compressed representation → Decoder) with probabilistic modeling (from Bayesian statistics). The key difference from a standard autoencoder is that VAEs learn a probability distribution over the compressed space rather than single fixed points.

Encoder
  • Compresses input data into a lower-dimensional space
  • Rather than a single point, outputs distribution parameters (mean and variance)
Decoder
  • Takes a sampled point from the distribution
  • Reconstructs (or generates) data from that low-dimensional representation
Why Probabilistic?

Because the compressed space follows a known probability distribution (typically Gaussian / Normal), you can sample random points from it and the Decoder will produce realistic outputs. This makes generation predictable and controllable — you know the shape of the space you are sampling from. [+]

Key Points
  • VAE = Encoder compresses to a probability distribution; Decoder reconstructs from samples of that distribution.
  • The probabilistic (Gaussian) latent space enables controlled, predictable generation.
  • VAEs produce slightly blurry outputs compared to GANs but train more stably.
F
Model Optimization Techniques
F
F1 Prompt Engineering

Prompt Engineering is the practice of carefully crafting the inputs you provide to a Foundation Model in order to guide it toward better, more accurate, or more appropriately formatted outputs. It requires no changes to the model itself — you are working entirely through the model's input interface.

A well-structured prompt typically contains some combination of these four components:

Instruction
Defines the task — "Summarize the following text in three bullet points."
Context
Background information that guides the model's interpretation — "You are a senior AWS solutions architect."
Input Data
The raw content for the model to act on — the document, the code snippet, the question.
Output Indicator
Specifies the desired format — "Respond in JSON," "Use bullet points," "Limit to 100 words."
Key Points
  • Prompt engineering changes inputs — it never modifies model weights or architecture.
  • The four components (instruction, context, input data, output indicator) are the standard prompt anatomy.
  • It is the fastest and cheapest optimization technique — no compute or data costs.
F2 Fine-Tuning and RLHF

Fine-tuning takes a pretrained Foundation Model and continues training it on a smaller, domain-specific dataset. This adjusts the model's weights so it performs better at a particular task or within a particular domain — narrowing a general-purpose model into a specialist. Fine-tuning is supervised learning applied to a pretrained model.

Instruction Tuning
  • Fine-tune using (prompt, ideal response) pairs
  • Teaches the model how it should respond to instructions
  • Example: Prompt Tuning, FLAN-style training
RLHF
  • Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
  • Humans rate or compare model responses
  • A reward model is trained on those preferences
  • The LLM is fine-tuned to maximize human approval ratings
Key Points
  • Fine-tuning modifies model weights using a smaller, task-specific dataset.
  • RLHF uses human ratings as the reward signal to align model output with human preferences.
  • Fine-tuning is more expensive than prompt engineering but produces deeper behavioral change.
F3 Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is a technique that improves model outputs by providing relevant external knowledge at inference time. Before generating a response, the system retrieves related documents or data from an external knowledge base and includes them in the prompt as additional context.

RAG solves a fundamental limitation of Foundation Models: their knowledge is frozen at training time. A RAG system can access up-to-date, private, or domain-specific information that the model was never trained on.

RAG vs. Fine-Tuning — Critical Distinction

RAG does NOT change model weights. It provides external context at inference time. Fine-tuning permanently changes the model's parameters. For exam scenarios: if the question is about incorporating new or private data without retraining, the answer is RAG.

Key Points
  • RAG = retrieve relevant documents → inject into prompt → model generates informed response.
  • No weight modification — RAG operates entirely at inference time.
  • Best for: incorporating private data, up-to-date information, or reducing hallucination with grounding.
  • Optimization hierarchy: Prompt Engineering (cheapest/fastest) → RAG → Fine-Tuning (most expensive, deepest change).
G
AWS AI/ML Infrastructure
G
G1 The Three-Layer AWS AI Stack

AWS organizes its AI and ML offerings into three distinct layers, each targeting a different level of abstraction and a different type of user. Understanding these layers helps you choose the right AWS service for a given scenario.

ML Frameworks
For practitioners who need full control — build, train, and deploy custom models. Amazon SageMaker is the flagship service at this layer.
AI/ML Services
Pre-built, task-specific AI services accessible via API — no ML expertise required. NLP, vision, speech, search, recommendations.
Generative AI
Foundation Model access and generative AI applications. Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Q, SageMaker JumpStart.
G2 ML Framework Layer: Amazon SageMaker

Amazon SageMaker is AWS's comprehensive ML platform for practitioners who need to build and deploy custom models. It covers the full ML lifecycle: data labeling, feature engineering, training at scale, model evaluation, deployment, and monitoring.

Core SageMaker Capabilities

Build — managed Jupyter notebooks, data preparation, feature store. Train — distributed training on managed compute clusters. Deploy — real-time endpoints, batch transform, serverless inference.

G3 AI/ML Services Layer

These are pre-built, task-specific AI services accessible via simple API calls. Each service encapsulates a trained model and exposes it through an interface that requires no ML knowledge to use — ideal for application developers who want AI capabilities without building models from scratch.

Amazon Comprehend
NLP insights: entity recognition, sentiment analysis, topic modeling, key phrase extraction
Amazon Translate
Neural machine translation across 75+ language pairs
Amazon Textract
Document data extraction and OCR — extracts text, tables, and forms from scanned documents
Amazon Lex
Build chatbot and voice interfaces. Uses ASR (speech-to-text) + NLU (intent understanding). Powers Alexa.
Amazon Polly
Text-to-speech synthesis. Converts written text to natural-sounding audio for voiceovers and accessibility.
Amazon Transcribe
Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) — converts spoken audio to text. Supports multiple languages and custom vocabulary.
Amazon Rekognition
Image and video analysis — object detection, facial analysis, content moderation, celebrity recognition
Amazon Kendra
Intelligent enterprise search powered by ML — understands natural language queries against internal documents
Amazon Personalize
Real-time personalized recommendations — the same technology powering Amazon.com recommendations
AWS DeepRacer
Reinforcement learning platform — autonomous 1/18th scale race car used for RL experimentation and learning
Key Distinctions to Memorize

Lex = chatbots (ASR + NLU, understands intent) · Transcribe = speech-to-text only (ASR) · Polly = text-to-speech · Rekognition = images and video · Textract = documents and forms · Kendra = enterprise search

G4 Generative AI Layer

The Generative AI layer provides access to Foundation Models and AI assistant experiences.

Amazon Bedrock
Managed FM access — Anthropic Claude, Meta Llama, Mistral, Amazon Titan, and more via a single API. No infrastructure management.
SageMaker JumpStart
Pre-built ML solutions and one-click deployable models. Accelerates development with templates and example notebooks.
Amazon Q
AI assistant for business — answers questions using your organization's internal data and knowledge bases.
Amazon Q Developer
AI-powered code generation, code review, and debugging assistant for software developers.
Key Points
  • Three layers: ML Frameworks (build custom models) → AI Services (pre-built APIs) → Generative AI (FMs and AI assistants).
  • SageMaker = full ML lifecycle for practitioners. Managed services = drop-in AI for developers.
  • Amazon Bedrock = single API for multiple FM providers. Amazon Q = business AI assistant. Q Developer = coding assistant.
  • Know which service maps to which task — this is frequently tested on AWS exams.
H
Advantages of AWS AI Solutions
H
H1 Why Build AI on AWS

AWS's value proposition for AI is built around eliminating the friction that typically slows AI deployment — infrastructure setup, model selection, scaling, and security. The core advantages make AWS a compelling platform for rapidly deploying AI without heavy upfront investment.

Faster Development
Prebuilt services and APIs mean teams ship AI features in days rather than months. No need to train models from scratch.
Scalability
Global infrastructure automatically scales inference capacity from a handful of requests to millions per second.
Cost Efficiency
Pay-as-you-go pricing — no upfront capital expenditure on GPU clusters. Pay only for what you consume.
Flexibility
Wide access to models — from open-source (Meta Llama) to proprietary (Anthropic Claude) — through Bedrock and SageMaker.
Integration
Seamless connectivity with the rest of the AWS ecosystem — S3, Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, and more.
Security
Enterprise-grade encryption, IAM-based access control, VPC isolation, audit logging, compliance certifications.
Exam Framing

AWS enables rapid AI deployment without heavy infrastructure investment. When exam questions describe scenarios that need fast deployment, broad model access, or elasticity — AWS managed AI services are the answer.

I
Cost Considerations in AWS AI
I
I1 The Cost-Performance Trade-off Framework

AWS AI is not free, and every architectural decision involves trade-offs between cost, performance, and availability. Understanding these trade-offs helps you make informed architecture decisions and answer cost-optimization exam questions.

Responsiveness
Latency vs. Cost — lower latency (faster responses) requires more compute, which costs more.
Availability
Uptime vs. Cost — high availability (99.99% uptime) requires redundant infrastructure, increasing cost.
Redundancy
Reliability vs. Cost — backup systems and failover mechanisms add cost but prevent costly outages.
Performance
Compute Power vs. Pricing — larger, more capable models cost more per inference than smaller, faster models.
AWS AI Pricing Models

Token-based Pricing — pay per input/output token consumed. Common for Bedrock FM APIs. Cost scales directly with usage volume. Provisioned Throughput — reserve dedicated model capacity upfront for predictable, high-volume workloads. Higher fixed cost, lower per-request cost at scale. Custom Models — fine-tuning and hosting custom models incurs higher training and deployment costs than using shared managed models.

Key Points
  • Every performance improvement (lower latency, higher availability, more model capability) increases cost.
  • Token-based pricing = pay per use. Provisioned Throughput = reserved capacity for consistent high-volume workloads.
  • Custom/fine-tuned models = highest cost due to training compute and dedicated hosting.
  • Optimal AWS AI architecture balances cost, performance, and scalability — not just one dimension.
Module Summary
  • AI ⊃ ML ⊃ Deep Learning ⊃ Generative AI — a nested hierarchy where each layer is a more specific form of the one above. Generative AI creates; traditional ML analyzes or predicts.
  • ML quality is data quality — the pipeline (Collect → Prepare → Train → Evaluate → Iterate) is only as strong as the data flowing through it. Inference is the deployment phase where the trained model serves predictions.
  • Foundation Models are pretrained at internet scale using self-supervised learning, making them general-purpose starting points. Optimization techniques (Prompt Engineering → RAG → Fine-Tuning) vary in cost and depth of model change; RAG does not modify weights.
  • AWS AI services span three layers: SageMaker (build custom models), managed AI APIs (pre-built task-specific services), and the Generative AI layer (Bedrock, Amazon Q). Knowing which service maps to which task is essential for AWS exams.
  • Every AWS AI architectural decision involves cost trade-offs across latency, availability, throughput, and model size. Token-based and provisioned throughput pricing models each suit different usage patterns.
AWS-AI MasterClass · Module 01 · Fundamentals of Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence
